question: Adam Fox was one of the first members of an informal literary discussion group associated with what University?
evidence_a:
TITLE: Adam Fox
[0] Adam Fox (1883 – 1977), Canon, was the Dean of Divinity at Magdalen College, Oxford.
[1] He was one of the first members of the "Inklings", a literary group which also included C.S. Lewis and J.R.R. Tolkien.
[2] Between 1938 and 1942 he was Professor of Poetry.
[3] Later he became Canon of Westminster Abbey and he is buried there in Poet's Corner.
[4] He was also Warden (Headmaster) of the famous Radley College.
evidence_b:
TITLE: Inklings
[0] The Inklings was an informal literary discussion group associated with the University of Oxford, England, for nearly two decades between the early 1930s and late 1949.
[1] The Inklings were literary enthusiasts who praised the value of narrative in fiction and encouraged the writing of fantasy.

[2] MedStar Georgetown is co-located with the Georgetown University Medical Center and is affiliated with the Georgetown University School of Medicine.
[3] Its clinical services represent one of the largest, most geographically diverse, and fully integrated healthcare and delivery networks in the area.

question: Which was published or broadcast first, The Graveyard Book of Phineas and Ferb?
evidence_a:

[1] "The Graveyard Book" traces the story of the boy Nobody "Bod" Owens who is adopted and raised by the supernatural occupants of a graveyard after his family is brutally murdered.
evidence_b:
TITLE: Phineas and Ferb
[0] Phineas and Ferb is an American animated musical comedy television series.
[1] Originally broadcast as a one-episode preview on August 17, 2007 and again previewed on September 28, 2007, the series officially premiered on February 1, 2008 on Disney Channel, and follows Phineas Flynn and his British stepbrother Ferb Fletcher on summer vacation.
[2] Every day, the boys embark on some grand new project, which annoys their controlling sister, Candace, who frequently tries to reveal their shenanigans to her and Phineas' mother, Linda Flynn-Fletcher, and less frequently to Ferb's father, Lawrence Fletcher.
[3] The series follows a standard plot system; running gags occur every episode, and the b-plot almost always features Phineas and Ferb's pet platypus Perry the Platypus working as a spy ("Agent P") for OWCA (the Organization Without a Cool Acronym), to defeat the latest scheme of Dr. Heinz Doofenshmirtz, a mad scientist driven largely by a need to assert his evilness.
[4] Sometimes, other villains scoff at his level of evil.
[5] The two plots intersect at the end to erase all traces of the boys' project just before Candace can show it to their mother.
[6] This usually leaves Candace very frustrated.

question: What was the 54th edition the 2009 UEFA Champions League Final, Europe's premier club football tournament , played?
evidence_a:
TITLE: 2008–09 UEFA Champions League
[0] The 2008–09 UEFA Champions League was the 54th edition of Europe's premier club football tournament and the 17th edition under the current UEFA Champions League format.
[1] The final was played at the Stadio Olimpico in Rome on 27 May 2009.
[2] It was the eighth time the European Cup final has been held in Italy and the fourth time it has been held at the Stadio Olimpico.
[3] The final was contested by the defending champions, Manchester United, and Barcelona, who had last won the tournament in 2006.
[4] Barcelona won the match 2–0, with goals from Samuel Eto'o and Lionel Messi, securing The Treble in the process.
[5] In addition, both UEFA Cup finalists, Werder Bremen and Shakhtar Donetsk featured in the Champions League group stage.
evidence_b:
TITLE: 2009 UEFA Champions League Final
[0] The 2009 UEFA Champions League Final was played on 27 May 2009 at the Stadio Olimpico in Rome, Italy.
[1] The match determined the winners of the 2008–09 season of the UEFA Champions League, a tournament for the top football clubs in Europe.
[2] The match was won by Barcelona of Spain, who beat England's Manchester United 2–0.
[3] Samuel Eto'o opened the scoring in the 10th minute, and Lionel Messi added another goal 20 minutes from the end to earn Barcelona a historic treble of La Liga, the Copa del Rey and the Champions League, a feat never before achieved by a Spanish club.
[4] The match was refereed by Swiss referee Massimo Busacca.

question: Which band formed first, Swervedriver or Incubus?
evidence_a:
TITLE: Swervedriver
[0] Swervedriver are an English alternative rock band formed in Oxford in 1989 around core members Adam Franklin and Jimmy Hartridge.
[1] Between 1989 and 1998, the band released four studio albums and numerous EPs and singles despite a considerable flux of members, managers, and record labels.
[2] By 1993 the band’s lineup had settled with Franklin on vocals/guitar, Hartridge on guitar, Jez Hindmarsh on drums, and Steve George on bass.
[3] They had emerged with a heavier rock sound than their shoegaze contemporaries, and over the next five years it evolved to include elements of psychedelia, classic pop, and indie rock.
[4] In 2008, the band reunited for touring purposes.
[5] They released their first new single in fifteen years in September 2013, and their first full-length album in seventeen years in March 2015.
evidence_b:
TITLE: Incubus (band)
[0] Incubus is an American rock band from Calabasas, California.
[1] The band was formed in 1991 by vocalist Brandon Boyd, lead guitarist Mike Einziger, and drummer Jose Pasillas while enrolled in Calabasas High School and later expanded to include bassist Alex "Dirk Lance" Katunich, and Gavin "DJ Lyfe" Koppell; both of whom were eventually replaced by bassist Ben Kenney and DJ Kilmore respectively.

question: What line featured characters from a DC Comic creator by Bob Kane and Bill Finger?

evidence_b:
TITLE: Batman in film
[0] The fictional superhero Batman, who appears in American comic books published by DC Comics, has appeared in various films since his inception.
[1] Created by Bob Kane and Bill Finger, the character first starred in two serial films in the 1940s, "Batman" and "Batman and Robin".
[2] The character also appeared in the 1966 film "Batman", which was a feature film adaptation of the 1960s "Batman" TV series starring Adam West and Burt Ward, who also starred in the film.
[3] Toward the end of the 1980s, the Warner Bros. studio began producing a series of feature films starring Batman, beginning with the 1989 film "Batman", directed by Tim Burton and starring Michael Keaton.
[4] Burton and Keaton returned for the 1992 sequel "Batman Returns", and in 1995, Joel Schumacher directed "Batman Forever" with Val Kilmer as Batman.
[5] Schumacher also directed the 1997 sequel "Batman & Robin", which starred George Clooney.
[6] "Batman & Robin" was poorly received by both critics and fans, leading to the cancellation of "Batman Unchained".

question: Also known as the Great Action, what other name did this event go by?
evidence_a:
TITLE: Helmut Rauca
[0] Helmut Rauca (3 November 1908 – 29 October 1983) was the Holocaust perpetrator instrumental in the murder of more than 10,000 Jews from the Kaunas Ghetto, Lithuania, during World War II.
[1] He was a member of "Einsatzgruppe A" in the rank of "Hauptscharführer" (master sergeant).
[2] As the Gestapo Jewish Affairs Specialist, Rauca was responsible for the selection of about one-third of the Ghetto inmates including men, women, and children, to be killed during the "Große Aktion" known as the Kaunas massacre of October 29, 1941 perpetrated at the remote Ninth Fort on the outskirts of Kaunas.
evidence_b:
TITLE: Kaunas massacre of October 29, 1941
[0] The Kaunas massacre of October 29, 1941 also known as the Great Action was the largest mass murder of Lithuanian Jews.

evidence_b:
TITLE: League of Nations
[0] The League of Nations (abbreviated as LN in English, "La Société des Nations " ] abbreviated as SDN or SdN in French) was an intergovernmental organisation founded on 10 January 1920 as a result of the Paris Peace Conference that ended the First World War.
[1] It was the first international organisation whose principal mission was to maintain world peace.
[2] Its primary goals, as stated in its Covenant, included preventing wars through collective security and disarmament and settling international disputes through negotiation and arbitration.
[3] Other issues in this and related treaties included labour conditions, just treatment of native inhabitants, human and drug trafficking, the arms trade, global health, prisoners of war, and protection of minorities in Europe.
[4] At its greatest extent from 28 September 1934 to 23 February 1935, it had 58 members.

question: Did the the Battle of Stones River occur before the Battle of Saipan?
evidence_a:
TITLE: Battle of Saipan
[0] The Battle of Saipan was a battle of the Pacific campaign of World War II, fought on the island of Saipan in the Mariana Islands from 15 June to 9 July 1944.
[1] The Allied invasion fleet embarking the expeditionary forces left Pearl Harbor on 5 June 1944, the day before Operation Overlord in Europe was launched.
[2] The U.S. 2nd Marine Division, 4th Marine Division, and the Army's 27th Infantry Division, commanded by Lieutenant General Holland Smith, defeated the 43rd Infantry Division of the Imperial Japanese Army, commanded by Lieutenant General Yoshitsugu Saito.
evidence_b:
TITLE: Battle of Stones River
[0] The Battle of Stones River or Second Battle of Murfreesboro, was fought from December 31, 1862, to January 2, 1863, in Middle Tennessee, as the culmination of the Stones River Campaign in the Western Theater of the American Civil War.
[1] Of the major battles of the Civil War, Stones River had the highest percentage of casualties on both sides.
[2] Although the battle itself was inconclusive, the Union Army's repulse of two Confederate attacks and the subsequent Confederate withdrawal were a much-needed boost to Union morale after the defeat at the Battle of Fredericksburg, and it dashed Confederate aspirations for control of Middle Tennessee.

evidence_b:
TITLE: Cooley v. Board of Wardens
[0] Cooley v. Board of Wardens, 53 U.S. 299 (1852), was a United States Supreme Court case in which the Court held that a Pennsylvania law requiring all ships entering or leaving Philadelphia to hire a local pilot did not violate the Commerce Clause of the Constitution.
[1] Those who did not comply with the law had been required to pay a fee.
[2] "It is the opinion of a majority of the court that the mere grant to Congress of the power to regulate commerce, did not deprive the States of power to regulate pilots, and that although Congress had legislated on this subject, its legislation manifests an intention, with a single exception, not to regulate this subject, but to leave its regulation to the several states," wrote Justice Curtis for the majority.

question: Who aided the Colorado forces in the Uruguayan war?
evidence_a:
TITLE: Siege of Salto
[0] The Siege of Salto occurred during the Uruguayan War, from 22 until 28 November 1864, when Brazilian forces (under Marquis of Tamandaré) and Colorado forces (under Venancio Flores) attempted to capture the city of Salto in Uruguay from Uruguayan Army defenders.
evidence_b:
TITLE: Uruguayan War
[0] The Uruguayan War (10 August 1864 – 20 February 1865) was fought between Uruguay's governing Blanco Party and an alliance consisting of the Empire of Brazil and the Uruguayan Colorado Party, covertly supported by Argentina.
[1] Since its independence, Uruguay had been ravaged by intermittent struggles between the Colorado and Blanco factions, each attempting to seize and maintain power in turn.
[2] The Colorado leader Venancio Flores launched the Liberating Crusade in 1863, an insurrection aimed at toppling Bernardo Berro, who presided over a Colorado–Blanco coalition (fusionist) government.
[3] Flores was aided by Argentina, whose president Bartolomé Mitre provided him with supplies, Argentine volunteers and river transport for troops.

question: Are  Chrysalis and Look both women's magazines?
evidence_a:
TITLE: Chrysalis (magazine)
[0] Chrysalis: A Magazine of Women's Culture was a feminist publication produced from 1977 to 1980.
[1] The self-published magazine was founded by Kirsten Grimstad and Susan Rennie at the Woman's Building in downtown Los Angeles.
[2] "Chysalis" grew from Grimstad and Rennie's editorial work on the self-help resource books, The New Woman's Survival Catalog and The New Woman's Survival Sourcebook.
[3] "Chrysalis" distinguished itself from other feminist publications through an organic integration of politics, literature, cultural studies, and art.
[4] The magazine was produced through a collective process that grew out of the feminist practice of consciousness-raising.
[5] Unusually broad in scope, "Chrysalis" did not substitute breadth for quality.
[6] The authors, poets, essayists, and researchers contributing to the magazine reveals a veritable who's who of towering intellects of the feminist movement: black lesbian activist Audre Lorde; the magazine's poetry editor, Robin Morgan, who later served as editor of Ms. from 1990-1993: award winning poet Adrienne Rich; novelist Marge Percy; artist Judy Chicago; science fiction writer Joanna Russ; art critic Lucy Lippard, plus Mary Daly, Dolores Hayden, Andrea Dworkin, Marilyn Hacker, Arlene Raven, and Elizabeth Janeway.
[7] Over a three-year span, the all volunteer staff produced ten issues before they were forced to disband in 1981 due to financial difficulties.

[3] The flowers are small, white or pink, with a five-lobed tubular corolla which is produced in mid-spring.

[2] He was captain of the Iran national football team from 2006 to 2009, and currently is the fourth most capped Iranian International after Ali Daei, Javad Nekounam and Ali Karimi.
[3] From the Bank Melli youth academy, he joined Persepolis and after his performance in the 1998 FIFA World Cup was transferred to Hamburger SV in the Bundesliga, where he played for eight seasons.
[4] He usually played as a right winger or full-back.
[5] He was known for his crossing, speed and dribbling.
[6] He announced his retirement on 14 March 2013 from football world.
[7] His last match as a football player was against Sepahan in the Hazfi Cup final on 5 May 2013.
evidence_b:

[0] Hamburger Sport-Verein e.V. ] , commonly known as Hamburger SV, Hamburg or HSV ] , is a German sport club based in Hamburg, its largest branch being its football department.
[1] Although the current HSV was founded in June 1919 from a merger of three earlier clubs, it officially traces its origin to 29 September 1887 when the first of the predecessors, SC Germania, was founded.
[2] HSV's football team has the unique distinction of having played continuously in the top tier of the German football league system since the end of World War I.
[3] It is the only team that has played in every season of the Bundesliga since its foundation in 1963, at which time the team was led by German national captain Uwe Seeler.

question: Which band has released more albums with their original members, Sick Puppies or Third Eye Blind?
evidence_a:
TITLE: Sick Puppies
[0] Sick Puppies is an Australian rock band, formed in 1997.
[1] Sick Puppies rose to prominence in 2006 when their song "All the Same" was uploaded along with a video to YouTube.
[2] The video supported the Free Hugs Campaign which was launched in Sydney, and has since received over 77 million views on the website.
[3] This success followed up with "Dressed Up as Life" in 2007, which entered the "Billboard" 200 at number 181.
[4] Their third studio album, released on 14 July 2009, is titled "Tri-Polar".
[5] The band's fourth studio album "Connect" was released on 16 July 2013.
[6] They released their fifth studio album "Fury" on 20 May 2016 with new vocalist Bryan Scott after Shimon Moore left the band on October 2014.
evidence_b:
TITLE: Third Eye Blind
[0] Third Eye Blind is an American rock band formed in San Francisco in 1993.
[1] The songwriting duo of Stephan Jenkins and Kevin Cadogan signed the band's first major label recording contract with Elektra Records in 1996, which was later reported as the largest publishing deal ever for an unsigned artist.
[2] The band released their self-titled album, "Third Eye Blind", in 1997, with the band largely consisting of Jenkins (lead vocals, rhythm guitar), Cadogan (lead guitar), Arion Salazar (bass guitar), and Brad Hargreaves (drums).
[3] Shortly after the release of the band's second album in 1999, "Blue", with the same line-up, Cadogan was released from the band under controversial circumstances.

question: "Kapitän zur See" Ernst Lindemann was the commander of which one of two battleships built for Nazi Germany's "Kriegsmarine"?
evidence_a:
TITLE: Ernst Heinrich Lindemann
[0] Ernst Heinrich Lindemann (25 January 1833 in Kirchlengern – 8 May 1900 in Düsseldorf) was German politician and mayor of Essen, Dortmund and Düsseldorf.
[1] His grandson "Kapitän zur See" Ernst Lindemann was the commander of the battleship "Bismarck".
evidence_b:
TITLE: German battleship Bismarck
[0] Bismarck was the first of two "Bismarck"-class battleship s built for Nazi Germany's "Kriegsmarine".
[1] Named after Chancellor Otto von Bismarck, the primary force behind the unification of Germany in 1871, the ship was laid down at the Blohm & Voss shipyard in Hamburg in July 1936 and launched in February 1939.
[2] Work was completed in August 1940, when she was commissioned into the German fleet.
[3] "Bismarck" and her sister ship "Tirpitz" were the largest battleships ever built by Germany, and two of the largest built by any European power.
